answer:
Definition

An intermediate File Upload system handles multi-file uploads with progress tracking, image resizing/optimization pipelines, resumable uploads for large files, virus scanning, signed URLs for secure access, and CDN integration. It goes beyond basic upload to address production concerns: what happens when a 500MB file upload fails at 80%? How do you serve uploaded images at different resolutions?

Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, the avatar upload pipeline demonstrates intermediate patterns: the user crops an image via Canvas API, the frontend compresses it to WebP before upload, Fastify streams it to Cloudinary with transformation presets (thumbnail 64x64, medium 256x256, original), and the API returns all three URLs. For CSV bulk import, a resumable upload mechanism tracks byte offsets so a failed 10MB upload can resume from where it stopped.

Code Example

// Multi-variant image upload with Cloudinary transformations
fastify.post('/api/upload/avatar', { preHandler: [authenticate] }, async (req, reply) => {
  const file = await req.file();

  // Validate magic bytes (not just mimetype)
  const buffer = await file.toBuffer();
  const fileType = await import('file-type');
  const type = await fileType.fileTypeFromBuffer(buffer);
  
  if (!type || !['image/jpeg', 'image/png', 'image/webp'].includes(type.mime)) {
    return reply.status(400).send({ error: 'Invalid image format' });
  }

  // Upload with eager transformations for multiple sizes
  const result = await cloudinary.uploader.upload(
    `data:${type.mime};base64,${buffer.toString('base64')}`,
    {
      folder: 'contactshub/avatars',
      public_id: `user_${req.user.id}_${Date.now()}`,
      eager: [
        { width: 64, height: 64, crop: 'fill', format: 'webp' },
        { width: 256, height: 256, crop: 'fill', format: 'webp' },
      ],
      eager_async: false,
    }
  );

  // Store all variants
  await prisma.user.update({
    where: { id: req.user.id },
    data: {
      avatarThumb: result.eager[0].secure_url,
      avatarMedium: result.eager[1].secure_url,
      avatarOriginal: result.secure_url,
    },
  });

  return {
    thumb: result.eager[0].secure_url,
    medium: result.eager[1].secure_url,
    original: result.secure_url,
  };
});

// Resumable CSV upload with progress tracking
fastify.post('/api/upload/csv/init', { preHandler: [authenticate] }, async (req) => {
  const uploadId = crypto.randomUUID();
  await redis.hset(`upload:${uploadId}`, {
    userId: req.user.id,
    totalBytes: req.body.fileSize,
    uploadedBytes: 0,
    status: 'in-progress',
  });
  return { uploadId };
});

fastify.post('/api/upload/csv/chunk', { preHandler: [authenticate] }, async (req) => {
  const { uploadId, offset } = req.query;
  const chunk = await req.file();
  const buffer = await chunk.toBuffer();

  // Append to temporary file
  const path = `/tmp/uploads/${uploadId}.csv`;
  await fs.appendFile(path, buffer);
  await redis.hincrby(`upload:${uploadId}`, 'uploadedBytes', buffer.length);

  const meta = await redis.hgetall(`upload:${uploadId}`);
  const progress = Math.round((meta.uploadedBytes / meta.totalBytes) * 100);

  return { progress, uploadedBytes: parseInt(meta.uploadedBytes) };
});

Debugging Narrative

In ContactsHub, avatar uploads worked in Chrome but produced corrupted images in Safari. Manoj traced the issue to the Canvas API's toBlob() method — Safari returned PNG blobs even when WebP was requested (Safari added WebP Canvas support only recently). The fix was to detect browser support: canvas.toBlob(callback, 'image/webp') and check the resulting blob's type — if it fell back to PNG, skip client-side compression and let Cloudinary handle the WebP conversion server-side. He added a utility function supportsWebPExport() that creates a test canvas and checks the output type.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallImpactMitigation
Validating only file extension/mimetypeRenamed .exe passes as .jpgValidate magic bytes with file-type library
Loading entire file into memory (toBuffer)OOM on large files (100MB+)Stream to Cloudinary; use chunked uploads for CSV
No cleanup of temporary files on failureDisk fills up with orphaned uploadsCron job deletes temp files older than 1 hour
Single image variant storedServing 4K original for 32px thumbnail wastes bandwidthGenerate multiple sizes via Cloudinary eager transforms

Validation Strategy

  • Magic bytes: Upload a .txt file renamed to .jpg — assert 400 rejection.
  • Multi-variant: Upload avatar — assert 3 URLs returned (thumb, medium, original) and all are accessible.
  • Resumable: Upload 50% of CSV, disconnect, resume — assert final CSV is complete and valid.
  • Cross-browser: Test avatar upload in Chrome, Firefox, Safari — assert consistent output format.

Definition

Debugging File Upload at intermediate level means diagnosing issues with streaming, memory management, CORS preflight for multipart, corrupted binary data, timeout on large uploads, and race conditions in concurrent uploads. These bugs are particularly tricky because they depend on file size, network speed, and browser-specific behaviors.

Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, medical certificate uploads failed silently for files between 1MB and 2MB. The server returned 200 but Cloudinary stored a 0-byte file. The root cause: Fastify's @fastify/multipart was configured with limits.fileSize: 2MB, but the limit check happened mid-stream — when the limit was hit, the stream was destroyed but the upload Promise still resolved (it didn't reject). The partial stream was piped to Cloudinary, resulting in a corrupted/empty file.

Code Example

// BUG: Stream truncation — file silently truncated at size limit
// BEFORE — stream destroyed but no error sent to client
fastify.post('/api/upload', async (req, reply) => {
  const file = await req.file(); // stream may be truncated
  const result = await uploadToCloudinary(file.file); // uploads partial file!
  return { url: result.secure_url }; // 200 OK with corrupted file
});

// AFTER — check for truncation via 'truncated' property
fastify.post('/api/upload', async (req, reply) => {
  const file = await req.file();
  const chunks = [];
  
  for await (const chunk of file.file) {
    chunks.push(chunk);
  }

  if (file.file.truncated) {
    return reply.status(413).send({
      error: `File too large. Maximum size: ${maxSize / 1024 / 1024}MB`,
    });
  }

  const buffer = Buffer.concat(chunks);
  const result = await uploadToCloudinary(buffer);
  return { url: result.secure_url };
});

// BUG: CORS blocks multipart uploads in production
// BEFORE — CORS allows application/json but not multipart
fastify.register(cors, {
  origin: 'https://hrms.example.com',
  methods: ['GET', 'POST', 'PUT', 'DELETE'],
  // missing: allowedHeaders for Content-Type multipart
});

// AFTER — explicitly allow multipart content type
fastify.register(cors, {
  origin: 'https://hrms.example.com',
  methods: ['GET', 'POST', 'PUT', 'DELETE'],
  allowedHeaders: ['Content-Type', 'Authorization'],
  credentials: true, // needed for cookie-based auth
});

// BUG: Upload timeout on Render (30s default)
// BEFORE — 15MB file takes 45 seconds on slow connection
// Server returns 504 Gateway Timeout

// AFTER — increase Fastify timeout for upload routes only
fastify.post('/api/upload/csv', {
  config: { rawBody: true },
  preHandler: [authenticate],
  handler: async (req, reply) => {
    req.raw.setTimeout(300000); // 5 minutes for this route only
    // ... handle upload
  },
});

Debugging Narrative

In the HR Leave System, PDF uploads of medical certificates worked locally but failed on Render with a 504 error for files over 5MB. Manoj initially suspected Cloudinary's upload limit, but the error came before the Cloudinary call. The issue was Render's reverse proxy, which has a 30-second request timeout by default. On slow connections, a 5MB upload took 35+ seconds. The fix had two parts: (1) increase the route-specific timeout to 5 minutes, and (2) switch to chunked uploads with progress tracking, so each chunk completes within the timeout window. He also added a client-side check: if the estimated upload time exceeds 25 seconds, automatically switch to chunked mode.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallSymptomDebug MethodFix
Stream truncation without error200 OK but corrupted fileCompare uploaded file size vs originalCheck file.file.truncated flag; return 413
CORS blocks multipart preflightUpload works in Postman, fails in browserNetwork tab shows OPTIONS request failedAllow Content-Type header in CORS config
Gateway timeout on large files504 error after 30 secondsServer logs show no error — proxy kills connectionChunked upload or increase proxy timeout
Concurrent uploads overwrite each otherTwo users upload simultaneously; one file lostCheck Cloudinary public_id collisionsInclude user ID + timestamp in public_id

Validation Strategy

  • Truncation: Upload a 3MB file with 2MB limit — assert 413 returned, not 200 with corrupted file.
  • CORS: Upload from production frontend domain — assert preflight OPTIONS returns 200 with correct headers.
  • Timeout: Throttle network to 100kbps, upload 5MB file — assert chunked upload succeeds.
  • Concurrent: Upload 2 files simultaneously from different users — assert both stored correctly with unique IDs.

Definition

Comparing Realtime Chat implementations evaluates the tradeoffs between WebSocket (Socket.io), Server-Sent Events (SSE), and Long Polling. Each approach has different characteristics for latency, scalability, browser support, and complexity. The choice depends on whether communication is bidirectional (chat needs it) or unidirectional (notifications don't), and the infrastructure constraints (load balancers, proxies, serverless environments).

Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, Socket.io was chosen for real-time notifications (leave approved/rejected) because the system already needed bidirectional communication: employees submit requests and managers respond. Socket.io's built-in room concept maps perfectly to team channels — each manager joins a room for their team, receiving only relevant leave requests. SSE was considered but rejected because managers also need to send approval/rejection actions in real-time.

Code Example

// Approach 1: Socket.io (HR Leave System)
// Server
import { Server } from 'socket.io';
const io = new Server(httpServer, { cors: { origin: '*' } });

io.use((socket, next) => {
  const token = socket.handshake.auth.token;
  try {
    socket.user = jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET);
    next();
  } catch { next(new Error('Unauthorized')); }
});

io.on('connection', (socket) => {
  // Join team room based on user's team
  socket.join(`team:${socket.user.teamId}`);
  
  socket.on('chat:message', async (data) => {
    const saved = await prisma.message.create({ data: { ...data, userId: socket.user.id } });
    io.to(`team:${socket.user.teamId}`).emit('chat:message', saved);
  });
});

// Approach 2: SSE (simpler, server-push only)
fastify.get('/api/notifications/stream', { preHandler: [authenticate] }, (req, reply) => {
  reply.raw.writeHead(200, {
    'Content-Type': 'text/event-stream',
    'Cache-Control': 'no-cache',
    Connection: 'keep-alive',
  });

  const onNotification = (data) => {
    reply.raw.write(`data: ${JSON.stringify(data)}\n\n`);
  };

  // Subscribe to user's notifications
  pubsub.subscribe(`user:${req.user.id}:notifications`, onNotification);

  req.raw.on('close', () => {
    pubsub.unsubscribe(`user:${req.user.id}:notifications`, onNotification);
  });
});

// Approach 3: Long Polling (fallback)
fastify.get('/api/messages/poll', { preHandler: [authenticate] }, async (req) => {
  const since = req.query.since || new Date(0);
  
  // Wait up to 30 seconds for new messages
  const timeout = 30000;
  const start = Date.now();
  
  while (Date.now() - start < timeout) {
    const messages = await prisma.message.findMany({
      where: { teamId: req.user.teamId, createdAt: { gt: new Date(since) } },
    });
    if (messages.length > 0) return { messages };
    await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 1000)); // poll every second
  }
  return { messages: [] }; // timeout, no new messages
});

Debugging Narrative

In the HR Leave System, Socket.io worked perfectly in development but disconnected every 60 seconds in production on Render. The issue was Render's reverse proxy closing idle connections after 60 seconds. Socket.io's default ping interval was 25 seconds with a ping timeout of 20 seconds — but the proxy didn't recognize WebSocket ping frames as activity. The fix was reducing the ping interval to 10 seconds and enabling the transport upgrade path: start with polling (which works through any proxy) and upgrade to WebSocket. This made the connection resilient to aggressive proxy timeouts.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallImpactMitigation
WebSocket blocked by corporate proxyChat fails for enterprise usersSocket.io auto-falls back to HTTP polling
SSE limited to 6 connections per domainMultiple tabs exhaust browser connection poolUse SharedWorker or BroadcastChannel to share one SSE connection
Long polling burns server resources1000 users = 1000 held connectionsUse Socket.io or SSE; long polling only as last resort
No auth on WebSocket handshakeAnyone can connect and listen to messagesVerify JWT in Socket.io middleware before next()

Validation Strategy

  • Latency: Send message via Socket.io — assert received by other client within 100ms.
  • Reconnection: Kill server, restart — assert client reconnects and receives missed messages.
  • Proxy: Deploy behind Nginx — assert WebSocket upgrade succeeds or falls back to polling.
  • Scalability: Connect 1000 clients — assert memory usage stays under 500MB with Socket.io adapter.

Definition

Designing a Realtime Chat system involves message persistence, delivery guarantees, presence indicators (online/offline/typing), room/channel management, message history with pagination, and handling offline messages. The architecture must handle concurrent users, message ordering, and the challenge of synchronizing state between clients connected at different times.

Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, Socket.io powers a team chat for leave discussions. When a manager needs clarification on a leave request, they open a chat thread attached to that request. Messages are persisted in PostgreSQL with Prisma, and Socket.io delivers them in real-time. Offline users see unread counts when they log back in, and typing indicators show when the other person is composing a response.

Code Example

// Complete chat system — Server (Fastify + Socket.io + Prisma)
io.on('connection', (socket) => {
  // Join leave-request chat room
  socket.on('chat:join', async (leaveRequestId) => {
    // Verify user has access to this leave request
    const leave = await prisma.leaveRequest.findFirst({
      where: {
        id: leaveRequestId,
        OR: [
          { employeeId: socket.user.id },
          { managerId: socket.user.id },
        ],
      },
    });
    if (!leave) return socket.emit('error', 'Access denied');

    socket.join(`leave:${leaveRequestId}`);

    // Send message history (last 50)
    const history = await prisma.chatMessage.findMany({
      where: { leaveRequestId },
      orderBy: { createdAt: 'desc' },
      take: 50,
      include: { sender: { select: { name: true, avatarThumb: true } } },
    });
    socket.emit('chat:history', history.reverse());

    // Mark messages as read
    await prisma.chatMessage.updateMany({
      where: { leaveRequestId, senderId: { not: socket.user.id }, readAt: null },
      data: { readAt: new Date() },
    });
  });

  // Handle new message
  socket.on('chat:message', async ({ leaveRequestId, text }) => {
    const message = await prisma.chatMessage.create({
      data: {
        leaveRequestId,
        senderId: socket.user.id,
        text,
      },
      include: { sender: { select: { name: true, avatarThumb: true } } },
    });

    io.to(`leave:${leaveRequestId}`).emit('chat:message', message);
  });

  // Typing indicator (debounced on client)
  socket.on('chat:typing', ({ leaveRequestId }) => {
    socket.to(`leave:${leaveRequestId}`).emit('chat:typing', {
      userId: socket.user.id,
      name: socket.user.name,
    });
  });

  // Presence: notify room when user disconnects
  socket.on('disconnect', () => {
    for (const room of socket.rooms) {
      socket.to(room).emit('user:offline', socket.user.id);
    }
  });
});

// React Chat Component
function ChatRoom({ leaveRequestId }) {
  const [messages, setMessages] = useState([]);
  const [typingUser, setTypingUser] = useState(null);

  useEffect(() => {
    socket.emit('chat:join', leaveRequestId);
    socket.on('chat:history', setMessages);
    socket.on('chat:message', (msg) => setMessages(prev => [...prev, msg]));
    socket.on('chat:typing', ({ name }) => {
      setTypingUser(name);
      setTimeout(() => setTypingUser(null), 2000);
    });

    return () => {
      socket.off('chat:history');
      socket.off('chat:message');
      socket.off('chat:typing');
      socket.emit('chat:leave', leaveRequestId);
    };
  }, [leaveRequestId]);
}

Debugging Narrative

In the HR Leave System, messages appeared out of order when two people sent messages simultaneously. Message A (sent at 10:00:00.100) appeared after Message B (sent at 10:00:00.050) because Socket.io delivered A first (lower network latency). The fix was to sort messages by their createdAt timestamp from the database, not by arrival order. On the client, each incoming message is inserted at the correct position using binary search on the timestamp, rather than simply appending to the array.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallImpactMitigation
No access control on room joinAny user can join any chat room by guessing IDVerify user's access to the resource before socket.join()
Messages not persisted before emitMessages lost if server crashes between emit and saveAlways await prisma.create() before io.emit()
No message ordering on clientMessages appear out of chronological orderSort by server timestamp, not arrival order
Typing indicator never clearsShows "typing..." forever if user closes tabAuto-clear after 2s timeout; clear on disconnect event

Validation Strategy

  • Ordering: Two clients send 10 messages each simultaneously — assert both see all 20 in chronological order.
  • Persistence: Send message → restart server → reconnect — assert message appears in history.
  • Access control: User not part of leave request tries to join chat — assert error emitted, no messages received.
  • Typing: Start typing, close tab — assert typing indicator clears on other client within 2 seconds.

Definition

Designing Search with Debounce creates a type-ahead search experience where the UI waits for the user to stop typing before sending an API request — preventing excessive API calls on every keystroke. At intermediate level, it adds result caching, search highlighting, recent search history, cancellation of stale requests, and empty-state handling. The debounce delay is typically 300-500ms.

Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, the contacts search bar uses a 300ms debounce with request cancellation. When the user types "Jo", waits 300ms, the API is called. If they continue typing "John" before the first response returns, the "Jo" request is cancelled via AbortController and a new request for "John" is sent after another 300ms pause. Results are cached so searching "Jo" again is instant, and matched text is highlighted in the results.

Code Example

// Custom useDebounceSearch hook with abort + cache
import { useState, useEffect, useRef, useCallback } from 'react';

function useDebounceSearch(searchFn, delay = 300) {
  const [query, setQuery] = useState('');
  const [results, setResults] = useState([]);
  const [loading, setLoading] = useState(false);
  const abortRef = useRef(null);
  const cacheRef = useRef(new Map());

  useEffect(() => {
    if (!query.trim()) {
      setResults([]);
      return;
    }

    // Check cache first
    if (cacheRef.current.has(query)) {
      setResults(cacheRef.current.get(query));
      return;
    }

    const timer = setTimeout(async () => {
      // Cancel previous in-flight request
      if (abortRef.current) abortRef.current.abort();
      abortRef.current = new AbortController();

      setLoading(true);
      try {
        const data = await searchFn(query, abortRef.current.signal);
        cacheRef.current.set(query, data);
        // Evict oldest if cache > 50 entries
        if (cacheRef.current.size > 50) {
          const oldest = cacheRef.current.keys().next().value;
          cacheRef.current.delete(oldest);
        }
        setResults(data);
      } catch (err) {
        if (err.name !== 'AbortError') console.error(err);
      } finally {
        setLoading(false);
      }
    }, delay);

    return () => clearTimeout(timer);
  }, [query, delay, searchFn]);

  return { query, setQuery, results, loading };
}

// Usage in ContactsHub
function ContactSearch() {
  const searchContacts = useCallback(async (q, signal) => {
    const res = await fetch(`/api/contacts/search?q=${encodeURIComponent(q)}`, { signal });
    return res.json();
  }, []);

  const { query, setQuery, results, loading } = useDebounceSearch(searchContacts);

  return (
    

       setQuery(e.target.value)} placeholder="Search contacts..." />
      {loading && }
      {results.map(c => (
        

          
        

      ))}
    

  );
}

// Server-side search endpoint (Fastify + MongoDB)
fastify.get('/api/contacts/search', { preHandler: [authenticate] }, async (req) => {
  const { q } = req.query;
  return db.collection('contacts').find({
    userId: req.user.id,
    $or: [
      { name: { $regex: q, $options: 'i' } },
      { email: { $regex: q, $options: 'i' } },
      { phone: { $regex: q, $options: 'i' } },
    ],
  }).limit(20).toArray();
});

Debugging Narrative

In ContactsHub, the search results sometimes showed results for a previous query. The user typed "John", results loaded, then typed "Jane" — but the "John" results appeared briefly before being replaced by "Jane" results. The race condition occurred because the "John" API response arrived after the "Jane" request was sent but before the "Jane" response returned. The fix was AbortController: when a new search starts, the previous request is aborted, ensuring its response handler never executes. Manoj also wrapped the state update in a check: if (!signal.aborted) setResults(data) as a safety net.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallImpactMitigation
No request cancellation on new keystrokeStale results flash before current resultsAbortController cancels previous in-flight request
Debounce too short (50ms)Still sends many requests on fast typing300-500ms is optimal for most search UIs
No minimum query lengthSingle-char search returns too many results, slowRequire minimum 2-3 characters before searching
Regex injection on serverUser sends .* as query, matches everythingEscape regex special chars or use text index

Validation Strategy

  • Debounce: Type 5 characters rapidly — assert only 1 API call (not 5).
  • Cancellation: Type "Jo", immediately type "Jane" — assert no flash of "Jo" results.
  • Cache: Search "John", clear, search "John" again — assert no API call on second search.
  • Highlighting: Search "an" — assert "an" is highlighted in "Jane Anderson".

Definition

Debugging Search with Debounce focuses on race conditions between requests and responses, stale closure bugs in debounce callbacks, memory leaks from uncancelled requests, incorrect cache invalidation, and server-side query performance issues. These bugs are timing-dependent, making them hard to reproduce consistently — they appear more on slow networks or with fast typists.

Use-Case — CRM Sales Dashboard

In Manoj's CRM Dashboard, the deal search had a bug where clearing the search input still showed the last search results instead of the full deal list. The debounce timer from the last keystroke was still pending when the input was cleared — it fired 300ms later with the previous query, overwriting the "show all" state. The fix was to cancel the debounce timer and abort any in-flight request when the query becomes empty.

Code Example

// BUG 1: Clearing input doesn't reset results
// BEFORE — pending timer fires after clear
const [query, setQuery] = useState('');
useEffect(() => {
  const timer = setTimeout(() => {
    if (query) fetchResults(query); // what if query was cleared?
  }, 300);
  return () => clearTimeout(timer);
}, [query]);
// Problem: clearing query to '' doesn't actively reset results

// AFTER — explicitly handle empty query
useEffect(() => {
  if (!query.trim()) {
    setResults([]); // reset to full list
    if (abortRef.current) abortRef.current.abort();
    return;
  }
  const timer = setTimeout(() => fetchResults(query), 300);
  return () => clearTimeout(timer);
}, [query]);

// BUG 2: Stale closure captures old query value
// BEFORE — debounce callback sees stale query
const debouncedSearch = useCallback(
  debounce(() => {
    fetchResults(query); // 'query' is stale closure!
  }, 300),
  [] // empty deps means query is always the initial value
);

// AFTER — pass query as argument, not from closure
const debouncedSearch = useMemo(
  () => debounce((q) => {
    fetchResults(q); // use argument, not closure
  }, 300),
  []
);

const handleChange = (e) => {
  const val = e.target.value;
  setQuery(val);
  debouncedSearch(val); // pass current value
};

// BUG 3: Cache shows stale results after editing a contact
// BEFORE — user edits "John Smith" to "John Doe", searches "Smith" — cached result still shows "John Smith"
// AFTER — invalidate search cache on any contact mutation
const editContact = async (id, data) => {
  await api.put(`/contacts/${id}`, data);
  cacheRef.current.clear(); // clear all search cache
  // Or selectively: delete entries where results contain this contact ID
};

Debugging Narrative

In the CRM Dashboard, users reported a bizarre bug: searching for a deal, editing it, then searching the same term again showed the old (pre-edit) data. The search results were cached by query string, and editing a deal didn't invalidate the cache. Manoj added a version counter that increments on any deal mutation — the cache key includes the version, so mutations effectively invalidate all cached search results. He considered selective invalidation (only clear cache entries containing the edited deal) but chose full invalidation for simplicity, since the cache rebuilds quickly from the API.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallSymptomDebug MethodFix
Stale closure in debounce callbackSearch always uses initial query valueconsole.log inside callback — shows old valuePass value as argument; don't rely on closure
Clearing input doesn't reset resultsOld results persist after backspacing to emptyCheck state after clearing inputExplicit setResults([]) when query is empty
Cache not invalidated on data mutationStale results after editing a recordSearch same term before/after edit — compareClear cache on mutations or use versioned cache keys
Memory leak from uncancelled requestsState updates on unmounted component warningReact DevTools console warningsAbortController + cleanup in useEffect return

Validation Strategy

  • Clear input: Type "John", then clear — assert results reset to empty/full list immediately.
  • Stale closure: Type "A", wait, type "B" — assert results are for "B" not "A".
  • Cache invalidation: Search "Smith", edit match, search "Smith" again — assert updated data shown.
  • Unmount safety: Start search, navigate away immediately — assert no "state update on unmounted component" warning.
